one_line_takeaway: A tutorial that reframes coding theory against RATIONAL adversaries via a Stackelberg game, showing (through the authors' prior results) that incentive-aware acceptance thresholds enable Sybil-resistant, non-zero data recovery even under an adversarial majority where classical honest-majority coding fails.
key_finding: Replacing the worst-case adversary with a rational one lets coding-based outsourcing achieve a non-zero probability of correct data recovery even when adversarial nodes are in the MAJORITY, recovering classical (decoding-failure) guarantees only in the limiting case where the adversary places no value on liveness. A surprising structural result is that the equilibrium MSE-vs-PA curve c_eta(alpha) depends only on the number of honest nodes ell and NOT on the number of adversarial nodes; consequently the framework is Sybil-resistant (a single honest node can suffice, and cloning adversarial identities does not change the equilibrium because only max(y) and min(y) drive both the acceptance rule and the estimator, and a rational adversary should control only one of them). Worked Example 1 shows the DC trading accuracy for liveness: optimal eta*=6.75 gives PA=0.807, MSE=10.07, versus the naive eta=2 baseline giving PA=0.37, MSE=2.10.
core_contribution: This is a tutorial/survey paper that introduces and synthesizes the authors' own 'game of coding' framework, a game-theoretic extension of coding theory to trust-minimized settings where honest nodes are NOT in the majority. Its distinctive contribution as a paper (versus the underlying results) is threefold: (1) it motivates the problem from decentralized machine learning (DeML) and blockchain-based computation outsourcing, arguing that incentive structures make a 'rational adversary' model more appropriate than the classical worst-case adversary; (2) it defines four requirements for computation outsourcing (low computational overhead, support for approximate computing, fast finality, resilience in adversarial-majority settings) and uses them in a comparison Table I that positions Game of Coding against Verifiable Computing, Optimistic Verification, and Coded/Redundant Computing, claiming Game of Coding is the only approach satisfying all four; and (3) it gives a pedagogical, worked-example exposition of the equilibrium. IMPORTANT: the actual technical results it reviews (equilibrium characterization, Sybil resistance, and the incomplete-information learning algorithms) are from the same authors' prior works, cited as [25] IEEE Trans. Inf. Theory 2025, [26] arXiv:2410.05540, and [27] arXiv:2502.07109 — this paper repackages and explains them rather than proving new theorems.
threat_model: A RATIONAL (not worst-case malicious) adversary in a trust-minimized decentralized outsourcing setting. A data collector (DC), typically a smart contract, outsources estimation of a value u to N external nodes; the adversary may control a MAJORITY of nodes (violating the classical honest-majority assumption). Adversaries are rewarded for accepted contributions and can only influence the DC's estimate if their submission is accepted, so they behave strategically to maximize utility rather than to cause arbitrary disruption. The interaction is modeled as a two-player Stackelberg game: the DC (leader) commits to an acceptance threshold eta, then the adversary (follower) best-responds by choosing a noise distribution g(.). Threats addressed: (i) denial-of-service where a worst-case adversary forces the DC to reject all inputs (loss of liveness); (ii) accuracy degradation of the estimate; and (iii) Sybil attacks where the adversary spawns many fake identities. An incomplete-information variant is also considered, where the DC does not know the adversary's utility function.
limitations: Stated by the paper's own Discussions/Open Problems section: (1) only REPETITION coding is analyzed; extension to general linear codes (acceptance rules, decoding, code-design criteria, richer adversary strategies) is open. (2) The (max+min)/2 estimator is shown near-MMSE-optimal only for N=2 nodes (one honest, one adversarial), up to an additive O((eta*Delta)^3 / M); the N>2 case is open, and it is not even clear that the MMSE estimator is always beneficial for the DC because reducing MSE can reduce PA. (3) 'Properness' problem: counterintuitively, INCREASING the number of honest nodes can DECREASE the DC's utility (shown in [26]); conditions guaranteeing monotone benefit are unresolved. (4) The incomplete-information analysis assumes a MYOPIC adversary; a nonmyopic adversary that manipulates the DC's learning process over rounds is unaddressed. Beyond the stated ones: the work is entirely theoretical with only illustrative examples (no datasets, no DeML deployment or experiments), and the clean analysis assumes uniform, symmetric, bounded honest noise and Delta << M.

datasets_used: None — purely theoretical paper; no empirical datasets. Only three closed-form illustrative examples using ell=1 honest node and honest noise uniform on [-1,1].
quantitative_results: Classical bounds cited as the problem baseline: MDS (K,N) code needs at least floor((N-K)/2)+1 honest nodes; repetition coding needs honest majority |H| >= |T|+1; Reed-Solomon (K,N) needs |H| >= |T|+K; Lagrange coded computing for degree-d polynomial needs |H| > |T|+(K-1)d
Example 1 (UAD = log MSE + (3/4) log PA, UDC = -MSE + 25*PA): Stackelberg equilibrium eta*=6.75 with PA=0.807 and MSE=10.07; naive eta=2 baseline yields (PA, MSE) = (0.37, 2.10)
Example 2 (UAD = log MSE + (1/4) log PA, UDC = PA/sqrt(MSE)): eta*=3.75 with equilibrium (PA, MSE) = (0.214, 6.52)
Example 3 (UAD = log MSE + (1/4) log(PA+0.3), UDC = -MSE + PA): eta*=2 with (PA, MSE) = (0, 4) — misaligned utilities collapse to zero acceptance at equilibrium

Structural result: the equilibrium curve c_eta(alpha) = h*_{eta,ell}(alpha)/(4 alpha) depends only on the number of honest nodes ell, not on the number of adversarial nodes (basis of Sybil resistance; analysis reduces to a single adversarial node)
MMSE result: for N=2 the (y1+y2)/2 estimator is optimal up to additive O((eta*Delta)^3 / M), vanishing as Delta << M
Motivating figures cited from other work: a single verifiable-computing server is limited to ~100 million gates; blockchain rollups secure >40 billion in assets
baselines_compared: Verifiable Computing [18]-[22] (ZK/validity proofs) — satisfies fast finality and adversarial-majority resilience but has high proof-generation overhead and is incompatible with approximate/real-valued computing

Optimistic Verification [23],[24] (fraud proofs / interactive bisection over Merkle-committed traces) — low overhead and adversarial-majority resilient but lacks fast finality (multi-day fraud windows) and is incompatible with approximate computing

Coded (Redundant) Computing [3]-[9] (repetition / error-correcting / Lagrange codes with median or algebraic decoding) — low overhead, supports approximate computing, fast finality, but NOT resilient in adversarial-majority (requires honest-majority-type conditions)
Game of Coding [25]-[27] (the proposed framework) — claimed in Table I to satisfy all four requirements simultaneously
